Numerical Linear Algebra

Solving Nonlinear Equations: Newton's Method in One and Many Dimensions

Solving Nonlinear Equations: Newton’s Method in One and Many Dimensions

Every method in the previous chapters solved linear problems: Ax=bAx=bAx=b, eigenvalue equations, least squares. This chapter steps outside that world to ask a more general question — how do we solve f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0 when fff isn’t linear at all, and no algebraic formula for the solution exists? The strategy turns out to be the same idea used throughout calculus for approximating hard problems with easy ones: replace the nonlinear function, locally, with something linear — a tangent line, or in higher dimensions, a tangent plane — and solve that instead. Do this repeatedly, and (under the right conditions) the sequence of approximations races toward the true root. By the end of the chapter, solving a system of nonlinear equations will come down to repeatedly solving a linear system, bringing the entire toolkit from the earlier chapters — Gaussian elimination, LU, QR — back into play.

The Root-Finding Problem

We want to solve f(x)=0.f(x) = 0.f(x)=0. For most functions that show up in practice, no closed-form solution exists, so we turn to numerical methods that approximate a root to any desired accuracy.

Before approximating anything, it helps to know a root actually exists in the region we’re searching. That guarantee comes from a familiar calculus fact, restated here in the language of root-finding.

Intermediate Value Theorem

Let f:[a,b]→Rf:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}f:[a,b]→R be continuous. If f(a)<0<f(b)f(a)<0<f(b)f(a)<0<f(b) or f(a)>0>f(b)f(a)>0>f(b)f(a)>0>f(b) — that is, if fff changes sign across the interval — then there exists c∈(a,b)c\in(a,b)c∈(a,b) with f(c)=0f(c)=0f(c)=0.

Why this matters

The IVT guarantees existence of a root wherever fff changes sign — it says nothing about the root’s exact value. But that existence guarantee is the theoretical bedrock underneath many root-finding methods (bisection being the most direct example), and it’s a useful sanity check even for the faster method this chapter focuses on: before iterating, it’s worth confirming a root is actually there to find.

Newton’s Method

Newton’s method

An iterative technique for approximating a solution of f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0. Starting from an initial guess x0x_0x0​, it produces a sequence x1,x2,…x_1,x_2,\dotsx1​,x2​,… intended to converge to a root x∗x^*x∗.

The central idea: replace fff, locally, by its tangent line at the current iterate.

Geometric picture

At the current point (xn,f(xn))(x_n, f(x_n))(xn​,f(xn​)): draw the tangent line to fff‘s graph there, find where that line crosses the xxx-axis, and use that crossing point as the next guess xn+1x_{n+1}xn+1​. If the tangent line is a good local stand-in for fff — which it is, whenever fff is smooth and xnx_nxn​ is reasonably close to a root — then xn+1x_{n+1}xn+1​ lands closer to the true root than xnx_nxn​ did, and repeating the process homes in on it.

Advantages and limitations

Advantages: often converges very rapidly near a root; for a simple root, convergence is quadratic (defined precisely below); simple to implement once fff and f′f'f′ are available. Limitations: requires f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) explicitly; fails or behaves erratically if f′(xn)=0f'(x_n)=0f′(xn​)=0 or is very small; can diverge from a poor starting guess; behaves badly near multiple roots or inflection points.

Newton’s method is fundamentally a local method — it works well once started close enough to a root, but offers no guarantee of converging from an arbitrary starting point. This is exactly the same caveat that applied to Rayleigh quotient iteration a few chapters ago: both methods trade global reliability for extremely fast local convergence.

Derivation of Newton’s Method

The tangent-line picture above has a precise algebraic counterpart via Taylor’s theorem. Expanding fff about the current iterate xnx_nxn​: f(x)=f(xn)+f′(xn)(x−xn)+12f′′(ξ)(x−xn)2,f(x) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x-x_n) + \frac12f''(\xi)(x-x_n)^2,f(x)=f(xn​)+f′(xn​)(x−xn​)+21​f′′(ξ)(x−xn​)2, for some ξ\xiξ between xxx and xnx_nxn​. When xxx is close to xnx_nxn​, the quadratic term is small relative to the linear one, so we drop it: f(x)≈f(xn)+f′(xn)(x−xn).f(x) \approx f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x-x_n).f(x)≈f(xn​)+f′(xn​)(x−xn​). This is precisely the tangent-line approximation. Setting x=xn+1x = x_{n+1}x=xn+1​ and demanding f(xn+1)≈0f(x_{n+1})\approx0f(xn+1​)≈0 (i.e., asking the tangent line, not fff itself, to hit zero): 0=f(xn)+f′(xn)(xn+1−xn)⟹xn+1=xn−f(xn)f′(xn).0 = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x_{n+1}-x_n) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}.0=f(xn​)+f′(xn​)(xn+1​−xn​)⟹xn+1​=xn​−f′(xn​)f(xn​)​.

Newton iteration formula

xn+1=xn−f(xn)f′(xn).x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}.xn+1​=xn​−f′(xn​)f(xn​)​.

Reading each step

Each Newton step: (1) evaluate f(xn)f(x_n)f(xn​) and f′(xn)f'(x_n)f′(xn​); (2) compute the correction Δxn=−f(xn)/f′(xn)\Delta x_n = -f(x_n)/f'(x_n)Δxn​=−f(xn​)/f′(xn​); (3) update xn+1=xn+Δxnx_{n+1} = x_n+\Delta x_nxn+1​=xn​+Δxn​. Writing it this way — as a correction added to the current guess — is deliberate, since it’s exactly the form that generalizes to systems later in this chapter.

Error Analysis and Convergence

The tangent-line approximation was only approximate, so it’s worth asking precisely how much error survives from one step to the next.

Let x∗x^*x∗ be a true root (f(x∗)=0f(x^*)=0f(x∗)=0), and define the error en=x∗−xne_n = x^*-x_nen​=x∗−xn​. Applying Taylor’s theorem to f(x∗)f(x^*)f(x∗), expanded about xnx_nxn​: 0=f(x∗)=f(xn)+f′(xn)(x∗−xn)+12f′′(ξ)(x∗−xn)2.0 = f(x^*) = f(x_n) + f'(x_n)(x^*-x_n) + \frac12f''(\xi)(x^*-x_n)^2.0=f(x∗)=f(xn​)+f′(xn​)(x∗−xn​)+21​f′′(ξ)(x∗−xn​)2. Dividing by f′(xn)f'(x_n)f′(xn​) (assuming it’s nonzero) and rearranging: x∗=xn−f(xn)f′(xn)−12f′′(ξ)f′(xn)(x∗−xn)2.x^* = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} - \frac12\frac{f''(\xi)}{f'(x_n)}(x^*-x_n)^2.x∗=xn​−f′(xn​)f(xn​)​−21​f′(xn​)f′′(ξ)​(x∗−xn​)2. The first two terms on the right are exactly the Newton update xn+1x_{n+1}xn+1​, so x∗=xn+1−12f′′(ξ)f′(xn)(x∗−xn)2⟹en+1=−12f′′(ξ)f′(xn)en2.x^* = x_{n+1} - \frac12\frac{f''(\xi)}{f'(x_n)}(x^*-x_n)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad e_{n+1} = -\frac12\frac{f''(\xi)}{f'(x_n)}e_n^2.x∗=xn+1​−21​f′(xn​)f′′(ξ)​(x∗−xn​)2⟹en+1​=−21​f′(xn​)f′′(ξ)​en2​.

This single equation is the entire story of Newton’s method’s speed: the new error is proportional to the square of the old error. If f′′f''f′′ stays bounded and f′(xn)f'(x_n)f′(xn​) stays away from zero near the root, the coefficient −12f′′(ξ)/f′(xn)-\frac12 f''(\xi)/f'(x_n)−21​f′′(ξ)/f′(xn​) stays bounded too, giving en+1≈Men2e_{n+1}\approx Me_n^2en+1​≈Men2​ for some constant MMM — the defining signature of quadratic convergence.

Local quadratic convergence of Newton’s method

Let f∈C2f\in C^2f∈C2 near a root rrr with f(r)=0f(r)=0f(r)=0 and f′(r)≠0f'(r)\neq0f′(r)=0. Then for x0x_0x0​ sufficiently close to rrr, Newton’s method converges to rrr quadratically: lim⁡n→∞en+1en2=f′′(r)2f′(r),so∣en+1∣≈∣f′′(r)2f′(r)∣∣en∣2 for large n.\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{e_{n+1}}{e_n^2} = \frac{f''(r)}{2f'(r)}, \qquad\text{so}\qquad |e_{n+1}| \approx \left|\frac{f''(r)}{2f'(r)}\right||e_n|^2 \text{ for large } n.limn→∞​en2​en+1​​=2f′(r)f′′(r)​,so∣en+1​∣≈​2f′(r)f′′(r)​​∣en​∣2 for large n.

This is precisely the same flavor of result seen with Rayleigh quotient iteration — there, cubic convergence came from letting a shift adapt to the current iterate; here, quadratic convergence comes from letting the tangent line adapt to the current iterate. Both are local methods that trade global guarantees for a convergence rate where each step roughly multiplies the number of correct digits, rather than merely adding a fixed number of them.

Simple roots vs. multiple roots

The hypothesis f′(r)≠0f'(r)\neq0f′(r)=0 says rrr is a simple root — the graph of fff actually crosses the axis there, rather than just touching it. At a multiple root, Newton’s method can still converge, but generally loses its quadratic speed and degrades to merely linear convergence.

Solving Systems of Nonlinear Equations

Everything above assumed a single unknown and a single equation. Real problems often involve nnn nonlinear equations in nnn unknowns simultaneously: f1(x1,…,xn)=0,f2(x1,…,xn)=0,…,fn(x1,…,xn)=0.f_1(x_1,\dots,x_n)=0, \quad f_2(x_1,\dots,x_n)=0, \quad\dots,\quad f_n(x_1,\dots,x_n)=0.f1​(x1​,…,xn​)=0,f2​(x1​,…,xn​)=0,…,fn​(x1​,…,xn​)=0. Collect the unknowns into X=[x1⋯xn]TX = \begin{bmatrix}x_1&\cdots&x_n\end{bmatrix}^TX=[x1​​⋯​xn​​]T and the equations into F(X)=[f1(X)⋯fn(X)]TF(X) = \begin{bmatrix}f_1(X)&\cdots&f_n(X)\end{bmatrix}^TF(X)=[f1​(X)​⋯​fn​(X)​]T, so that the problem becomes, compactly, F(X)=0F(X)=0F(X)=0 — formally identical to the scalar problem, just with vector-valued unknowns and outputs.

From 1D Newton to multivariable Newton

Rewriting the scalar update xk+1=xk−f(xk)/f′(xk)x_{k+1}=x_k-f(x_k)/f'(x_k)xk+1​=xk​−f(xk​)/f′(xk​) in terms of the correction Δx=xk+1−xk\Delta x = x_{k+1}-x_kΔx=xk+1​−xk​ gives f′(xk)Δx=−f(xk)f'(x_k)\Delta x = -f(x_k)f′(xk​)Δx=−f(xk​) — a form that generalizes directly, term by term:

Scalar (1D)System (nnnD)
Unknown xxxVector unknown XXX
Function f(x)f(x)f(x)Vector function F(X)F(X)F(X)
Derivative f′(x)f'(x)f′(x)Jacobian matrix
Correction Δx\Delta xΔxCorrection vector ΔX\Delta XΔX

The Jacobian matrix

Jacobian matrix

The Jacobian of FFF at XXX is the matrix of first partial derivatives: DF(X)=[∂f1∂x1⋯∂f1∂xn⋮⋱⋮∂fn∂x1⋯∂fn∂xn].DF(X) = \begin{bmatrix}\dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n}\\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial x_n}\end{bmatrix}.DF(X)=​∂x1​∂f1​​⋮∂x1​∂fn​​​⋯⋱⋯​∂xn​∂f1​​⋮∂xn​∂fn​​​​.

The Jacobian is the direct multivariable analogue of f′(x)f'(x)f′(x): it’s the matrix that gives the best linear approximation to FFF near a point, in exactly the sense that f′(xn)f'(x_n)f′(xn​) gave the best linear (tangent-line) approximation to fff in the scalar case.

Newton’s method for systems

Linearizing FFF near XkX_kXk​: F(Xk+ΔX)≈F(Xk)+DF(Xk)ΔX,F(X_k+\Delta X) \approx F(X_k) + DF(X_k)\Delta X,F(Xk​+ΔX)≈F(Xk​)+DF(Xk​)ΔX, and demanding the next iterate approximately satisfy F(Xk+1)≈0F(X_{k+1})\approx0F(Xk+1​)≈0 leads to F(Xk)+DF(Xk)ΔX=0⟹DF(Xk)ΔX=−F(Xk).F(X_k) + DF(X_k)\Delta X = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad DF(X_k)\Delta X = -F(X_k).F(Xk​)+DF(Xk​)ΔX=0⟹DF(Xk​)ΔX=−F(Xk​).

Newton’s method for systems

Given X0X_0X0​, repeat:

  1. Solve the linear system DF(Xk)ΔX=−F(Xk)DF(X_k)\Delta X = -F(X_k)DF(Xk​)ΔX=−F(Xk​) for ΔX\Delta XΔX.
  2. Update Xk+1=Xk+ΔXX_{k+1} = X_k+\Delta XXk+1​=Xk​+ΔX.

Notice what this reduces to: solving a nonlinear system has been converted, one step at a time, into solving a sequence of linear systems — exactly the problem the earlier chapters of this course spent so much effort solving well.

Why we do not explicitly invert the Jacobian

It’s tempting to write Xk+1=Xk−[DF(Xk)]−1F(Xk)X_{k+1} = X_k - [DF(X_k)]^{-1}F(X_k)Xk+1​=Xk​−[DF(Xk​)]−1F(Xk​) and read this as “compute the inverse, then multiply.” That is almost never how Newton’s method is implemented.

Numerical linear algebra principle

Do not explicitly compute a matrix inverse unless there is a specific reason to. This is the same guiding principle that steered the least-squares chapters away from forming ATAA^TAATA explicitly, and toward QR or SVD instead.

The reasons carry over directly:

  • Computational cost. Forming an explicit inverse is expensive, especially for large systems — far more work than simply solving one linear system.
  • Numerical stability. Explicit inversion can amplify roundoff error badly when the Jacobian is close to singular — precisely the swamping and ill-conditioning concerns raised back in the numerical-stability chapters.
  • A better alternative exists. It’s more efficient and more stable to solve DF(Xk)ΔX=−F(Xk)DF(X_k)\Delta X = -F(X_k)DF(Xk​)ΔX=−F(Xk​) directly, using the same tools built earlier in this course: Gaussian elimination, LU decomposition, QR-based solves, or — for large sparse problems — iterative linear solvers.

Variants and Practical Remarks

Two common relaxations of full Newton’s method trade some convergence speed for reduced cost per iteration — a tradeoff familiar from the shift-reuse tricks discussed with inverse iteration.

Inexact Newton iteration

Rather than solving DF(Xk)ΔX=−F(Xk)DF(X_k)\Delta X=-F(X_k)DF(Xk​)ΔX=−F(Xk​) exactly, solve it only approximately at each step. This is especially useful for large-scale problems where an exact linear solve is prohibitively expensive.

Frozen Jacobian method

Rather than recomputing the Jacobian at every iteration, reuse a previously computed one for several steps. This lowers the cost of each iteration (skipping both the Jacobian evaluation and, if reusing a factorization, the linear solve setup), at the cost of slower convergence — directly mirroring the earlier advice to factor A−σIA-\sigma IA−σI once and reuse it across repeated shifted-inverse-iteration steps.

Summary

Single equation. For f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0, xn+1=xn−f(xn)f′(xn),x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)},xn+1​=xn​−f′(xn​)f(xn​)​, converging quadratically when started near a simple root.

System of equations. For F(X)=0F(X)=0F(X)=0, DF(Xk)ΔX=−F(Xk),Xk+1=Xk+ΔX,DF(X_k)\Delta X = -F(X_k), \qquad X_{k+1} = X_k+\Delta X,DF(Xk​)ΔX=−F(Xk​),Xk+1​=Xk​+ΔX, with the Jacobian playing the role of the derivative, and each step requiring a linear solve rather than a division.

The key idea

In both the scalar and vector cases, Newton’s method works by replacing a nonlinear problem with its best local linear approximation, solving that easier linear problem, and repeating. This is the same theme that has run through this entire course in reverse: where earlier chapters solved linear problems as directly and stably as possible, this chapter shows how that same linear machinery becomes the engine for solving nonlinear ones.
